title: Auth token refresh returning empty body
root_cause: Redis session store ran out of memory. Writes silently failed — the endpoint returned HTTP 200 but did not persist the new token. Subsequent API calls failed with 401s, appearing as user-facing logout events.
business_impact: 8,000 users silently logged out over 4 hours. Support ticket volume spiked 300%.
resolution: Increased Redis maxmemory to 4GB and enabled LRU eviction policy. Added a write-verify step in the token refresh handler.
runbook: 1. Check Redis memory: redis-cli INFO memory. 2. Check eviction events: redis-cli INFO stats | grep evicted_keys. 3. Increase maxmemory and set LRU eviction. 4. Restart auth service.

title: Recommendations endpoint returning empty payload
root_cause: ML model serving layer (Triton) timed out internally but the API gateway caught the timeout and returned 200 with an empty array instead of propagating the 503.
business_impact: Recommendation widgets blank for 6 hours. Estimated 12% drop in click-through revenue.
resolution: Fixed gateway timeout handler to propagate 503. Added fallback to cached recommendations.
runbook: 1. Check Triton health: curl http://triton-server:8000/v2/health/ready. 2. Check gateway timeout config. 3. Enable cache fallback: kubectl set env deploy/recommendations USE_CACHE=true.

title: Checkout 404s staying just below pager threshold
root_cause: Canary deployment of the inventory service introduced a race condition in stock reservation. ~1.8% of checkout requests hit the race and returned 404 for valid cart items.
business_impact: Estimated $42,000 in lost checkout revenue. 1,800 failed purchase attempts.
resolution: Rolled back canary deployment. Added mutex lock around stock reservation. Lowered pager threshold to 1% for /api/checkout.
runbook: 1. Identify canary: kubectl get pods -l track=canary. 2. Roll back: kubectl argo rollouts abort <rollout-name>. 3. Verify error rate drops. 4. Investigate race condition in Jaeger.

title: Cart item fetch errors clustering below alert threshold
root_cause: DynamoDB hot partition for cart items belonging to a large B2B customer. Throttling errors retried silently by the SDK.
business_impact: Single enterprise customer affected. SLA breach triggered, $15,000 credit issued.
resolution: Added adaptive capacity for cart table. Implemented partition key sharding for large accounts.
runbook: 1. Check DynamoDB consumed capacity via CloudWatch. 2. Enable on-demand: aws dynamodb update-table --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST. 3. Implement partition sharding for accounts with >10k cart items.

title: Checkout latency spikes masking silent payment gateway retries
root_cause: Payment gateway (Stripe) returned intermittent 503s. Checkout service retried silently up to 3 times before succeeding. 0.3% of transactions double-charged due to non-idempotent retry.
business_impact: 312 customers double-charged. Full refund issued. Regulatory notification required.
resolution: Implemented idempotency keys for all Stripe API calls. Added explicit retry logging.
runbook: 1. Check Stripe status: https://status.stripe.com. 2. Search for duplicate charges in Stripe dashboard. 3. Issue refunds for duplicates. 4. Verify idempotency key implementation.

title: Product listing latency spike during cache warm-up
root_cause: Redis cache flush during maintenance caused thundering herd on the product database. All requests bypassed cache simultaneously, overwhelming Postgres.
business_impact: 30-minute degraded experience for all users. Estimated 8% bounce rate increase.
resolution: Implemented cache stampede protection. Added circuit breaker for cache miss thundering herd.
runbook: 1. Check Postgres connections: SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity. 2. Pre-warm cache: python scripts/cache_warm.py --category all. 3. Re-enable cache gradually with feature flag.

title: Detector wrongly flagged empty recommendations during scheduled cache warm-up
root_cause: The Detector agent flagged EMPTY_SUCCESS_RESPONSE on /api/recommendations during a routine 15-minute cache warm-up window. This is expected behaviour — the cache is intentionally cold during warm-up and returns empty arrays while populating.
business_impact: Zero business impact. False positive created unnecessary incident ticket and on-call page.
resolution: Analyzer agent correctly overruled the Detector by cross-referencing the scheduled maintenance window in the incident knowledge base. Added warm-up window suppression to the Detector's tool configuration.
runbook: 1. Check scheduled maintenance calendar before escalating. 2. Verify warm-up window: kubectl logs deploy/cache-warmer | grep 'warm-up started'. 3. If warm-up in progress, suppress alert for 20 minutes. 4. If empty responses persist after warm-up completes, escalate as TRUE_POSITIVE.
analyzer_reasoning: Cross-referenced with maintenance schedule. Empty 200s on /api/recommendations occurred within the 08:00-08:15 UTC scheduled cache warm-up window. This is documented expected behaviour. Recommend suppressing for 20 minutes and re-evaluating.

title: Sub-threshold errors during scheduled database maintenance flagged as silent failure
root_cause: Detector flagged 1.4% error rate on /api/cart during a 10-minute DynamoDB index rebuild. Errors were expected — the GSI was temporarily unavailable during rebuild. No actual silent failure.
business_impact: Zero business impact. False page triggered. Engineer interrupted for non-issue.
resolution: Analyzer retrieved INC-004 context and correctly identified this error pattern. Compared timestamps with the DynamoDB maintenance log. Confirmed false positive. Added maintenance-window error suppression.
runbook: 1. Check DynamoDB maintenance events: aws dynamodb describe-table --table-name cart-items. 2. Look for GSI rebuild status in CloudWatch. 3. If index rebuild in progress: suppress /api/cart error alerts for duration + 5 minutes. 4. Re-evaluate after rebuild completes.
analyzer_reasoning: Retrieved INC-004 — similar path and error pattern. Timestamp cross-reference shows errors began exactly when DynamoDB GSI rebuild was triggered at 14:32 UTC. Pattern matches documented maintenance behaviour. Overruling Detector finding.

title: Analyzer initially challenged but ultimately confirmed auth silent failure
root_cause: Detector flagged EMPTY_SUCCESS_RESPONSE on /api/auth/refresh. Analyzer initially challenged it, retrieving INC-007 (warm-up false positive pattern). However, second retrieval pass found INC-001 (true Redis OOM pattern). Analyzer noted: no maintenance window scheduled, Redis memory metrics showed 97% usage. Reversed challenge and confirmed TRUE_POSITIVE.
business_impact: 3,200 users silently logged out before detection. Faster than INC-001 due to multi-agent debate surfacing the issue sooner.
resolution: Same as INC-001. Redis maxmemory increase. The debate loop between agents reduced detection lag from 4 hours (Part 1) to 2 hours.
runbook: 1. Always check Redis memory before ruling out auth empty-response findings. 2. redis-cli INFO memory — if used_memory_rss > 90% of maxmemory, treat as TRUE_POSITIVE regardless of maintenance windows. 3. Escalate immediately.
analyzer_reasoning: Initial challenge based on warm-up pattern (INC-007). Second retrieval pass retrieved INC-001 — Redis OOM pattern. No scheduled maintenance found. Redis memory at 97%. Reversing challenge. Confirming Detector finding as TRUE_POSITIVE.

title: Analyzer overruled Detector on checkout latency — Detector was correct
root_cause: Detector flagged LATENCY_SPIKE_ON_SUCCESS on /api/checkout. Analyzer retrieved INC-006 (cache warm-up latency) and overruled. However, root cause was actually a new Stripe API degradation not in the knowledge base. The override was wrong — this was a genuine silent failure.
business_impact: 6-hour delay in detection due to incorrect Analyzer override. 89 customers double-charged. Knowledge gap in incidents.json caused the error.
resolution: Added INC-005 (Stripe payment retry pattern) to knowledge base. Implemented a rule: Analyzer overrides require at least 2 corroborating incidents, not just 1. Reporter now flags overrides as UNCERTAIN when only 1 incident supports the challenge.
runbook: 1. When Reporter marks a finding as UNCERTAIN: escalate to human review regardless. 2. Do not rely on single-incident Analyzer overrides for payment-path findings. 3. After any false override: add the missed pattern to incidents.json immediately.
analyzer_reasoning: Challenged based on INC-006 latency pattern similarity. This was incorrect — INC-005 (Stripe retry) was not yet in the knowledge base. Lesson: knowledge base gaps cause false overrides. Always add new incident types after postmortem.
